title: Facing Death with Courage
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 2-2-25 - Facing Death with Courage - Psalm 23 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: The Occasion and Our Discomfort with Death
subpoints: The sermon was occasioned by the passing of a beloved church member (Mrs. Bond)
American culture has sterilized and avoided the reality of death
Not talking about death does not stop it: mortality remains at 100%
Death is not a natural friend—it is the Bible's 'last enemy,' defeated only by Christ's resurrection

outline.title: Death Is Real, Unnatural, and Introduced by Sin
subpoints: God created humanity to live forever in His presence; death intruded through the fall
Physical death began the moment Adam and Eve fell—God's word proved true
Death is not romanticized in the Bible; it is called the 'last enemy'
The mission of Jesus was to reverse the devastation of the fall, including death itself

outline.title: The Shadow of Death Comes in Many Forms
subpoints: We face the shadow of death long before our final day: loss of health, loved ones, relationships, youth, independence, usefulness, and meaning
Each of these losses is a foretaste and reminder of the ultimate death to come
The older we get, the more vividly we sense what truly matters in life
Only what is done for Christ will last; all earthly accomplishments and possessions will perish

outline.title: We Never Face Death Alone
subpoints: Psalm 23:4—'You are with me; your rod and your staff, they comfort me'
No one on earth walks through that final door with us, but Jesus, who died and rose, does
Jesus faced death fully—crying out 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'—not as a stoic
His final words, 'Into your hands I commit my spirit,' model honest, trusting engagement with death

outline.title: Jesus Is Alive and Leads Us Through Death
subpoints: Hebrews 2: Jesus was made perfect through sufferings as the Captain of our salvation
Hebrews 12:1-2: He endured the cross for the joy set before Him—that joy includes His people
Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress: the river of death yields ground to stand on as we look to Christ
Believers die differently from unbelievers: with peace, acceptance, and a genuine walking through

outline.title: Practicing for Death by Living Well Now
subpoints: Practice dying by asking: What am I living for? What will last beyond death?
Nothing material, relational, or achieved can be taken beyond the grave
Only love for God and love for others is imperishable—these are what we were designed for
Accept the reality of your death, look straight through it, and see the Lord Jesus Christ on the other side

summary:
This sermon was preached in response to the death of a beloved church member, Mrs. Bond. Pastor Lyro sets aside his planned text and turns to Psalm 23 to address the congregation's grief and to provide a biblical framework for facing death with courage. He begins by observing that American culture has sterilized death—people rarely die at home, the dying are heavily medicated, and the subject is avoided. But ignoring death does not stop it. Mortality remains at 100%, and the death of a loved one is a natural occasion to consider our own.

The pastor argues that death is neither natural nor a friend. We were created to live forever with God; death intruded through the fall. When Adam and Eve sinned, God's word proved true: they died. Death is called in Scripture 'the last enemy,' and it took the resurrection of Jesus Christ to conquer it. The sermon catalogs the many ways the shadow of death falls over our lives before the final moment: loss of health, of loved ones, of youth, of independence, of meaning. Each is a foretaste of death and a reminder that only what is done for Christ will last.

The heart of the sermon is the comfort of Psalm 23:4: 'You are with me.' Jesus faced death fully—not as a stoic, but crying out Psalm 22 from the cross. Yet he committed his spirit into the Father's hands. That same Jesus, risen and alive, walks with every believer through the valley of the shadow of death. Drawing on Hebrews 2 and 12, Pastor Lyro shows that Christ endured the cross for the joy set before him—a joy that includes his people. Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress illustrates the same truth: as Christian and Hopeful enter the river of death and look to Christ, they find ground to stand on.

The sermon closes with an invitation to 'practice dying' by living now for what is eternal—to love God and love others, because only these are imperishable. Accepting the reality of death, staring through it to Christ on the other side, is the only way to face our last enemy with true and honest courage.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Psalm 23

I know the bulletin said one passage, but we actually are going to be turning to Psalm 23. So if you grab your Bible and turn to Psalm 23. Psalm 23, a Psalm of David.

The Lord is my shepherd; I shall not want. He makes me to lie down in green pastures. He leads me beside the still waters. He restores my soul. He leads me in the paths of righteousness for his name's sake. Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. For you are with me, your rod and your staff, they comfort me. You prepare a table before me in the presence of my enemies. You anoint my head with oil, my cup runs over. Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me all the days of my life and I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father pray that you speak to us today through your word. I pray that you help me to say what is helpful according to your word, pray that your spirit be present with us, point us to Christ, for asking His name, amen.

## Introduction: A Change of Plans

In all the years that I have been a pastor, I can remember only once changing what I am going to preach on the spur of the moment. If you know me, I am not a spur-of-the-moment person. I am a rigid person. I am not at all inflexible in what I do.

But I can't think of another Sunday when one of our members went to the ultimate worship service in the presence of the Lord. The Hunters have lost Mrs. Bond. The Claims family has also experienced loss, speaking of the loss of a grandchild in the womb of their daughter as well. I think this gives an opportunity to take a step back and see what the Scriptures say about death. And I think Psalm 23 gives us a good place to start.

## Death Is Real and Unnatural

We are not generally comfortable talking about death, especially in the American culture. We have sterilized death where people generally don't die at home anymore. Usually when they die, they are medicated to the point where they're not fully present anymore. The thing is that not talking about death and ignoring your death will not stop it from happening. As I checked, mortality is still at 100%. Every life on earth ends in death unless our Lord returns.

And the death of a loved one gives us a natural opportunity to consider our own mortality. Death is not a friend. Just ask somebody whose loved one just died. Death is not a friend. When people finally muster up the courage to talk about death, they tend to romanticize it. They talk vaguely about release from pain or going to a better place and being reunited with loved ones. And even though these things are true, the Bible never portrays death as a friend. Death in the Bible is called the last enemy. It took the resurrection of Jesus Christ to conquer that last enemy.

Death is the ultimate and final loss. It feels unnatural and wrong because it is unnatural and wrong. We were created by God to live forever. And death is not what God intended for His world. The mission of the Lord Jesus Christ, God incarnate, was to reverse the mess that the fall created. You remember in the fall, God created Adam and Eve perfectly to live with Him forever. And had not Adam and Eve fallen, they would have lived with God forever. And yet Satan tempted Eve with challenging the Word of God, and they fell. God's word proved to be true: when they ate of that fruit, they died.

The Lord Jesus Christ says in John chapter 10, verse 10-11, I have come that they may have life and that they may have it more abundantly. I am the good shepherd, the good shepherd gives his life for the sheep. And we have life more abundantly because of the Lord Jesus Christ.

## The Many Shadows of Death

We are people who are always facing the shadows of death as the Psalmist says in Psalm 23. We don't face death just at the end of life. Throughout our lives we face what David calls the shadow of death. A shadow brings the looming sense that the thing that projects the shadow is approaching.

The valley of the shadow of death takes many different forms. A loss of health is a shadow of that death to come. Loss of a loved one is another shadow of death. When death comes to those we love, we feel the shadow of death keenly upon us. But we also experience loss when a relationship ends for any reason, especially if it ends by betrayal. We've experienced the shadow of death in our loss of youth—the years pile up, the hair turns white, the wrinkles form, the body starts breaking down. Loss of independence is another reach of that shadow. Loss of usefulness is another tentacle of that death to come. Loss of meaning is another area where that shadow comes.

I have yet to meet a person at their death's door who says, "I'm so glad that I worked 100 hours a week for all those years. I'm so glad I climbed the corporate ladder that high." Because when death comes, we tend to be a little more tuned to what actually has meaning in life. As the sign out front has often said: only one life, 'twill soon be past; only what's done for Christ will last.

## We Never Face Death Alone

But we know this, brothers and sisters: we never go through death alone. Do you get that? At that moment where Mrs. Bond's heart started beating super fast to the point it couldn't function anymore and stopped beating altogether—she wasn't alone. The Lord Jesus was with her.

Jesus faced death for you and he faced his death with you. Isolation comes with all suffering, but it is even more pronounced when we face death. No one on earth will go through that door with you. No one, except the one who died for you and came back to life for you.

David says in verse 4: Yea, though I walk through the valley of the shadow of death, I will fear no evil. For you are with me. Our friends and relatives cannot go with us as we die. But the one who is closer than a brother promises to never leave us nor forsake us.

Jesus has firsthand knowledge of what we face in death. God himself will be with you in that moment when death stretches its fingers towards us. He's there and he walks with us. I've seen people die—I've seen believers die and I've seen unbelievers die. In my experience there's a significant difference. There's a calm, there's an acceptance, there's an actual walking through that door by the believer.

We face death, but we face it like Jesus did. Jesus was not a stoic when he died. In the Synoptic Gospels, Matthew, Mark and Luke, they quote Jesus screaming from the cross the first verse of Psalm 22: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me? He looked at death right in the eye, felt keenly its pain, felt its degradation, felt its horror. Then what did he do? He trusted his heavenly Father. As he said: into your hands I commit my spirit.

When the Lord says, "Into your hands I commit my spirit," I don't think these words were calm, cool and collected. They were the words of a man who was fully engaged with his troubles, fully engaged with his God, and bringing the two together in honest meeting. Right there, in your hands I trust my spirit—we find the collision of need and joy before the Lord. And that's exactly where Mrs. Bond was at that very moment where her heart stopped beating.

## Jesus Leads Us Through Death

This Jesus is alive and he's able to help us face death with faith. He has gone through death itself and he will lead us through it.

In Hebrews chapter 2, the Holy Spirit says, for it was fitting for him, for whom are all things and by whom are all things, in bringing many sons to glory, to make the captain of their salvation perfect through sufferings.

And then in Hebrews chapter 12 it says, therefore we also, since we are surrounded by so great a cloud of witnesses, let us lay aside every weight and the sin which so easily ensnares us, and let us run with endurance the race that is set before us, looking unto Jesus, the author and finisher of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and has sat down at the right hand of the throne of God.

For the joy set before him. The cross wasn't the joy. The joy is fellowship forever with his Father and his church through the cross. He saw Mrs. Bond. He saw all our beloved ones and said, that's the joy I'm going through this cross for.

We don't have to shrink back and pretend we're not going to die. We don't have to pretend that death doesn't hurt. It hurts. But we can entrust our soul to our Heavenly Father, just as Jesus did.

John Bunyan in his classic Pilgrim's Progress ends with a dramatic scene of the crossing of a river. The river is death itself and the two pilgrims, Christian and Hopeful, are about to cross. Christian began to sink and crying out to his good friend Hopeful said, I sink in deep waters. The bills are going over my head. All his waves go over me. To which Hopeful said, be of good cheer. Jesus Christ makes you whole. And with that Christian broke out with a loud voice: Oh, I see him again, and he tells me, when you pass through the waters I will be with you, and through the rivers they shall not overflow you. Then they both took courage and the enemy was after that as still as a stone until they were gone over.

That's exactly how Mrs. Bond crossed the river—by looking at Christ who received her with open arms on the other side.

## Practicing for Death by Living Well Now

So practice dying and practice living. Now I don't want you to start actually practicing dying. What I mean is this: we can start preparing ourselves for death right now by asking, what am I living for?

Are you living for what will last forever or for what will die with you? You came into the world with nothing and you will leave the world with nothing. You can't take your resources, you can't take your achievements, you can't take your possessions, you cannot take your spouses, you cannot take your children, you cannot take your grandchildren, you cannot take your friends with you. These are all good, God's good gifts to you. But if you're living for the gifts and not for the Giver, you will find that when you die, all you have lived for will perish with you.

When we experience hardship and grapple with the death of our dreams, the death of our hopes and the death of our desires, we will find out that only love for God and others is imperishable—because that's what we were designed to do. Our Lord says there are two things we're designed to do: to love God and to love others.

Accept the reality of your death. You will die. Unless our Lord returns before then, you will die. In our world, people work hard at not facing their eventual and certain deaths. But if we are to walk through the valley of the shadow of death and fear no evil, we must look straight at the shadows and the approaching death. Look at death and look through death—stare death right in the face and see, beyond the shadow, the light that dawns after the darkness: the Lord Jesus Christ Himself.

## Closing Prayer

Father in heaven, thank you that you are a good God. Thank you that you are a God who saves us and welcomes us into your presence. We pray that you be with us as we mourn and grieve. We pray that we would be able to do that in you, knowing that death is the last enemy and that enemy has been conquered by Christ. Thank you that you also sent us the Comforter, the Holy Spirit, and we pray that He will be doing His work in our hearts, for asking Jesus' name. Amen.
